report_name: Armis Proposes 'Release-Age Policy' to Defend Against Zero-Day Supply Chain Attacks

attack_patterns.name: Compromise Software Supply Chain
attack_patterns.description: Attackers publish malicious packages to public repositories, exploiting the 48-72 hour time gap before detection to achieve widespread compromise of developer workstations and CI/CD pipelines.

attack_patterns.name: Compromise Software Dependencies and Development Tools
attack_patterns.description: Threat actors use techniques like typosquatting, dependency confusion, or account takeovers to publish malicious packages to public repositories like NPM.

malware.name: Mini Shai-Hulud
malware.description: A worm cited as a recent example of a rapid software supply chain attack that exploited the 48-72 hour window before detection.

tools.name: axios
tools.description: A legitimate NPM package that was compromised in March 2026, cited as an example of a software supply chain attack.

identities.name: Software Development Organizations
identities.description: A class of organizations that develop software using open-source components, which are affected by the supply chain process vulnerability.

infrastructure.name: Public Package Repositories
infrastructure.description: Public software repositories like NPM and PyPI are used by attackers to distribute malicious packages as part of supply chain attacks.

relationships.target_name: Compromise Software Dependencies and Development Tools

relationships.source_name: Compromise Software Dependencies and Development Tools
